title: Put It In The World
statement:
Empty editor. The last task in the course.

There isn't a single new tag here. Everything you need, you've covered.

The hard part is the scope: until now each task asked you about one thing. This one asks about all of them at once — and it won't tell you which it's checking.

The page sits in the main folder. There's a subfolder pages with more pages, and a subfolder images with the photo.

Build it so you could publish it tomorrow.
constraints: The club is called Dobrich Photo Club
Menu: Home (index.html) · Walks (in pages) · Gallery (another site)
Main part: What we do, a sentence, a photo from images
Bring with you: a camera, comfortable shoes, water
Meeting times: a table with meeting Thursday 19:00, walk Sunday 07:00, darkroom Saturday 10:00
A note on gear: a sentence where the words aperture and shutter are VISIBLE in angle brackets
On the side, Nearby: two links into pages
Join us: a form with name and email, email required, a button
At the bottom: Written by Ivan. All rights reserved.
The page is ready to publish — it says everything about itself

checks.t13: There is exactly one main content
checks.t15: The navigation is in the top part
checks.t17: There is something on the side
checks.t19: There is exactly one main heading
checks.t24: The menu is a list of three links
checks.t25: There are at least five links
checks.t28: There is a link into a subfolder
checks.t29: Every link says its own name
checks.t39: Every row starts with a header cell
checks.t43: The fields are tied to their labels
checks.t51: There is text at the bottom

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the page yourself.
why.not-closed:
Some tag is opened and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.

The page is large: the table, the form and the nested parts are where this happens most easily.
why.no-doctype: The page doesn't say what kind of document it is. The first line of every HTML file.
why.no-lang:
The page doesn't say what language it's in, or says it empty.

A screen reader has to decide how to pronounce the words. Without a language it guesses — and reads nonsense confidently.
why.no-charset: The alphabet isn't declared, or it's empty. The first non-Latin letter will show you.
why.no-title:
The page has no name, or it's empty.

This is the first thing in search results and the only thing in bookmarks.
why.no-viewport:
The page doesn't say how to display on a phone.

Without it the phone shrinks the whole thing and the visitor reads with two fingers. More than half of them will open it exactly that way.
why.no-og:
The page doesn't say how it should look when shared, or the values are empty.

Send the address in a chat and a bare link shows up. You need at least the two basics: what it's called and which picture.
why.no-landmarks: The top part, the substance or the bottom part is missing. The substance is exactly one.
why.no-nav: There is no navigation in the top part.
why.no-article:
There is no standalone thing in the main content.

The description of the club means something even lifted out of the page.
why.no-aside:
There is nothing on the side in the main content.

The neighbouring pages aren't part of the club's description. They sit beside it.
why.wrong-nesting:
The nesting isn't right, or a nameless box is left over.

The thing on the side doesn't live inside the article — that would claim the neighbouring pages are part of the text about the club.

And every box here has a real name available. You don't need a nameless one.
why.h1-count:
There isn't exactly one main heading, or the heading is empty.

One per page. Two means the page is about two things at once.
why.few-h2: There aren't three subheadings. What we do, the neighbouring pages and joining are three separate parts.
why.few-h3: There aren't three sub-subheadings. What to bring, the times and the note on gear are parts INSIDE the description.
why.heading-order:
The headings skip a level.

From the main one you step down to the next, not over it. A blind visitor reads the page like a table of contents — skip a level and they wonder what they missed.
why.nav-not-list: The menu isn't a list of three links inside navigation.
why.few-links: There aren't five links with addresses. Three in the menu and two on the side make five.
why.empty-href: There is a link with an empty address.
why.no-external:
There is no link to another site with a full address.

The gallery isn't your file — its address starts with a protocol and a domain.
why.no-subfolder:
There is no link into a file in a subfolder.

The pages for the walks, the darkroom and the archive are in pages. You're in the main folder — so first you step into it.
why.no-link-name: There is a link with no name.
why.no-alt: The image has no description, or it is empty.
why.no-src:
The image doesn't say which file, or the address is empty.

It's in the images folder, and you're in the main one.
why.few-lists: There aren't three lists. The menu, what to bring and the neighbouring pages make three.
why.empty-li: There is an empty list item.
why.br-instead: You've used line breaks. Every list item takes its own line.
why.few-rows: The table doesn't have four rows. Three meetings and the row with the column names.
why.few-th:
There aren't six header cells.

Three along the top and one at the start of each row. Meeting isn't a value — it's the name of the row.
why.no-row-names:
The rows don't start with a header cell.

Take the cell 19:00 and name it. You need two addresses: the column above and the row to the left.
why.no-caption: The table has no name, or it's empty. Times change — say when these apply.
why.few-fields: There aren't two fields in the form.
why.no-labels:
There is a field with no label tied to it.

A word next to a field isn't a label. A label is a link.
why.no-email-type: There is no email field. Left as free text, it accepts anything.
why.no-required: Nothing is required. Someone sends an empty form and you get nothing.
why.no-name: There is a field with no name, or an empty one. A field with no name arrives nowhere.
why.no-button: There is no button. The form fills in but never leaves.
why.no-entities:
The angle brackets aren't visible on the page.

The note talks ABOUT settings in brackets — so the brackets have to be there in the text. Written the way your hand wants to, they aren't text: the browser takes them for the start of a tag and runs them.

And don't forget that every character name ends with a semicolon.
why.autoplay:
You've left something to start on its own.

Your browser blocks it — which is why it looks fine. On someone else's it won't.
why.layout-table:
You've put something into the table that isn't data.

A table isn't a grid for arranging things. Ask about the cell: what does it MEAN?
why.fake-heading:
You have a heading made out of a bolded paragraph.

On screen it does the job. But a blind visitor pulls up a list of headings and your line isn't in it.

no-og: Copy the page address and send it to yourself in a chat. What shows up on the other side?
Lesson 65 gave tags that tell someone else's site how to display your page.
You need at least the two basics: what it's called and which picture to show.
heading-order: Read only the headings, top to bottom. Is a level skipped anywhere?
The main one is one. Below it are the three big parts. Below those are the parts inside the description.
Three levels, in order, no skipping.
no-subfolder: Look at where you are and where the other pages are.
You're in the main folder. The walks, the darkroom and the archive are in a subfolder.
So first you step into it: its name, a slash, then the file.
no-row-names: Take any cell with a time and say it out loud: what is this?
You need two things — the column and the row. So both of them name something.
Meeting, Photo walk, Darkroom aren't values. They're row names.
no-entities: Read the note on gear on screen. Is all of it there?
What's missing wasn't deleted — it ran. The browser took it for a tag.
Lesson 23: you don't write the character, you write its name. And it ends with a semicolon.
wrong-nesting: Read only the tags out loud, top to bottom.
Do you hear "article, inside it something on the side"? Ask what that claims.
And count the nameless boxes. There's nothing here you need one for.

solution.why:
There's no single solution here. There are twenty small ones you already know separately.

And that's the exam. Until now every task told you what it was about: paths, tables, forms. You knew where to look.

Now you don't. The page is large and everything invisible you have to remember yourself — the language, the alphabet, the width for phones, the picture when shared, the image description, the label-to-field link, the header cell at the start of every row.

Not one of those things shows on screen. Every one of them is paid for by somebody else: the person on a phone, the blind visitor, the search engine, whoever you sent the link to in a chat.

That's why this course was about them, not about the tags. There are thirty tags and they take a week. The question "who can't use this" takes longer.
steps: Start with the head. It's where the page says what it is: language, alphabet, name, width for phones, picture when shared.
Split the body into three: top, substance, bottom.
The top holds the club's name and the menu. The menu is a list of links in navigation — one to a file beside you, one in a subfolder, one out.
The substance is one. Inside it, the club's description is a standalone thing.
In it: a sentence, a described photo, a list of what to bring, a table of times, a note on gear.
In the table, remember that names run in two directions, and that it has a name of its own.
In the note, the brackets have to be VISIBLE — so they aren't written as characters.
The neighbouring pages sit BESIDE the description, not in it.
Joining is a section of this page: a form with two labels tied to their fields, correct kinds, a required email, a button.
The copyright stays at the bottom.
Finally read only the headings top to bottom. Is a level skipped anywhere?
solution.code:
Dobrich Photo Club

  • Home
  • Walks
  • Gallery

What we do

We meet every Thursday, walk on Sundays and print on the first Saturday of the month.

Bring with you

  • A camera, any camera
  • Comfortable shoes
  • Water

Meeting times

Regular meetings, autumn 2025
What Day Time
Meeting Thursday 19:00
Photo walk Sunday 07:00
Darkroom Saturday 10:00

A note on gear

Write your settings as <aperture> and <shutter> in the shared sheet.

Nearby

  • The darkroom
  • Old photos

Join us

Written by Ivan. All rights reserved.
solution.note:
This is a page you can publish.

Before you do, run it through the validator from lesson 67, then press F12. The first will tell you what you wrote wrong; the second will show you what the browser fixed on your behalf.

Then open it on your phone. All the invisible things you just wrote suddenly become visible: the name in the tab, the width, the picture when shared.

That's why they were there.
